era_context: The final year of the Second Punic War (218–201 BCE), which has devastated the Mediterranean for nearly two decades. Rome is on the verge of final victory over Carthage. In the east, the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is expanding. Egypt under Ptolemy V is weakening. The Han Dynasty in China is consolidating power after the civil wars of the Chu-Han period. This is a year of transition and resolution in multiple regions.

events.title: Battle of Zama
events.description: The decisive final battle of the Second Punic War, fought between Scipio Africanus (Roman general) and Hannibal (Carthaginian general) near the town of Zama in North Africa. Scipio's superior tactics, particularly his use of cavalry and his ability to exploit gaps in Hannibal's formation, resulted in a decisive Roman victory. Hannibal's army was routed, and Carthage was forced to sue for peace. The battle marked the end of Carthage's challenge to Roman dominance and established Rome as the preeminent Mediterranean power. Hannibal escaped to exile, and Carthage was reduced to a minor regional state. The battle is considered one of the most significant military engagements in ancient history.
events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary sources confirm the Battle of Zama in 202 BCE. Livy and Polybius wrote within ~150 years. The date is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Fourth Syrian War concludes
events.description: The Fourth Syrian War (219–217 BCE), fought between the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III and the Ptolemaic Empire under Ptolemy IV, formally concluded around 202 BCE. Although Antiochus III had been victorious in several engagements, the war ended inconclusively, and both powers were exhausted. The war had demonstrated the weakness of both empires and set the stage for the Fifth Syrian War (202–195 BCE), which would result in Seleucid control of the Levant. The Fourth Syrian War is sometimes conflated with the Fifth in ancient sources, leading to confusion about dating.
events.certainty_note: The dating of the Fourth Syrian War is uncertain. Some sources place its conclusion in 217 BCE, others in 202 BCE. The year 202 BCE is assigned here by convention, but the actual date may vary by ±5 years.
cross_references: Led to -200-001 (Fifth Syrian War / Battle of Panium)

events.title: Han Dynasty: Kangxi's reign; consolidation of power
events.description: Emperor Kangxi of the Han Dynasty (r. 157–141 BCE) continued his reign during this period. The early Han Dynasty was consolidating power after the civil wars of the Chu-Han period (206–202 BCE), which had ended with the establishment of Han rule. Kangxi's reign was marked by relative stability, the continuation of the heqin policy with the Xiongnu, and the gradual expansion of Han influence. The year 202 BCE falls within the early consolidation phase of the Han Dynasty.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Chapters 10–12
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, Chapters 1–4

events.certainty_note: Kangxi's reign is well-attested, but the year 202 BCE does not correspond to any specific recorded event. The certainty is 'probable' based on the broader context of his reign.
cross_references: Part of broader Han Dynasty consolidation period (-206 to -141 BCE)

events.title: Ptolemy IV Philopator's death; succession of Ptolemy V
events.description: Ptolemy IV Philopator, who had ruled Egypt since 221 BCE, died around 202 BCE. He was succeeded by his young son, Ptolemy V Epiphanes (born c. 210 BCE), who was only about 8 years old at the time of his accession. The succession of a child king led to a period of regency and internal instability, with various court factions vying for power. This weakness invited intervention from neighboring powers, particularly the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III, and contributed to Egypt's loss of territorial control in the Levant. The death of Ptolemy IV marked the beginning of the decline of Ptolemaic power.
key_figures: Ptolemy IV Philopator (Pharaoh of Egypt, died)
Ptolemy V Epiphanes (Pharaoh of Egypt, succeeded)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy IV's death is dated c. 202 BCE, but some sources place it as late as 205 BCE. The date is uncertain by ±3 years. Ptolemy V's birth is dated c. 210 BCE, so his age at succession is approximately correct.
cross_references: Led to -200-001 (Battle of Panium, which took advantage of Egyptian weakness)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Fourth Syrian War is disputed. Some scholars argue that it ended in 217 BCE, not 202 BCE, and that the Fifth Syrian War (202–195 BCE) was a separate conflict. The distinction between the Fourth and Fifth Syrian Wars is sometimes unclear in ancient sources, and modern scholars debate whether they should be considered one continuous conflict or two separate wars. Additionally, the exact date of Ptolemy IV's death is uncertain; some sources place it in 205 BCE rather than 202 BCE.
historiographic_note: The year 202 BCE is well-documented for the Mediterranean world through Greek and Roman sources (Livy, Polybius, Appian). The Battle of Zama is one of the most famous battles in ancient history and is extensively documented. Chinese sources provide context for the Han Dynasty but are less detailed for this specific year. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history are underrepresented. Carthaginian sources are almost entirely lost.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Zama led to the Treaty of Zama
Death of Ptolemy IV and succession of the young Ptolemy V weakened Egypt and enabled Antiochus III's victory at Panium
Conclusion of the Fourth Syrian War led to the Fifth Syrian War and the Battle of Panium
